items.q: Does the free will defence explain natural evil?
items.correct: Its direct force concerns moral evil; to cover earthquakes and disease, defenders add further moves, such as Augustine's fallen world or Hick's claim that a law-governed, challenging world is needed for soul-making.
items.misconception: Free will straightforwardly explains why earthquakes and diseases occur.
items.random_wrong: The free will defence claims humans cause earthquakes by their choices.
items.why: Students stretch the defence beyond its scope; examiners reward knowing exactly which kind of evil each response targets.

items.q: Is Augustine saying that evil and suffering are not real?
items.correct: No - privation means evil is a lack or corruption of good, as blindness is an absence of sight; the suffering is real, but evil has no independent created existence.
items.misconception: Augustine taught that evil is just an illusion that nobody really experiences.
items.random_wrong: Augustine taught that God created evil as a separate substance.
items.why: 'Privation' is routinely misread as 'unreal'; the distinction protects God from being the author of evil without denying suffering.

items.q: Does a theodicy claim that evil is secretly good?
items.correct: No - a theodicy argues God has morally sufficient reasons for permitting genuinely bad things, such as preserving freedom or enabling growth; it does not deny evil's badness.
items.misconception: Theodicies say evil is really a good thing in disguise.
items.random_wrong: A theodicy is a prayer asking God to remove evil.
items.why: Conflating 'justified permission' with 'approval' caricatures the positions and weakens evaluation of them.

items.correct: The claim that God's omnipotence, God's perfect goodness and the existence of evil cannot all be true together
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The claim that God's omnipotence, God's perfect goodness and the existence of evil cannot all be true together. “The doctrine of the Trinity” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is an example of natural evil?
items.why: It's Suffering caused by an earthquake. “Theft” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: For Augustine, evil is best described as what?
items.correct: A privation of good rather than a substance God created
items.misconception: An equal and opposite force to God
items.random_wrong: A substance created by the devil
items.why: It's A privation of good rather than a substance God created. “An equal and opposite force to God” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does Hick's phrase 'vale of soul-making' claim?
items.correct: A world containing challenge and suffering enables persons to grow freely towards God
items.misconception: The world was created perfect and remains so
items.random_wrong: Souls are repeatedly reincarnated until perfect
items.why: It's A world containing challenge and suffering enables persons to grow freely towards God. “The world was created perfect and remains so” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
